case_name: State of New Hampshire v. Donald W. Little

segments.text: This is case 2025-0349, state of New Hampshire versus Donald W. Little.
For the record, the Chief Justice is disqualified from this case.
Attorney Garland, I understand that you've reserved one minute for buttle.
Yes, Your Honor.
Thank you, and may it please the court. My name is Sam Garland, and I represent the state of New Hampshire in this case.
I want to start by talking about a couple of things that I don't think are in dispute, just to home in on the issue that's before the court.
The basic facts are undisputed. The trial court concluded there are undisputed facts for the purposes of the motion to dismiss,
and those facts are that the defendant is subject to a protective order, that he had some sort of arrangement.
I'm not sure if it was court ordered or if it was by agreement, but it's not relevant for the crimes before the court today,
that he would send alimony checks to his ex-wife's law office, the victim's law office in Dover,
and that she would then come there to collect the checks, and that the specific events that gave rise to the charges here
are that he wrote Liar Cheat Thief on the memorandum line of one of those checks.
She came to her attorney's law office in Dover, got the check, brought it back to her home in Rockingham County,
and saw that message when she got back to Rockingham County.
So I don't think those facts are in dispute, and they perform the basis of both of the charges.
I also don't think it's in dispute, in this case, that for both of the charges,
violation of the protective order and stalking, contact is an element of the offense that the defendant himself has to commit.
So he has to engage in contact in a way that violates either of those statutes for the purposes of criminal liability.
And so consistent with fortune, consistent with 6021, the question in this case, and really the only question before the court is,
is contact an element of the offense that he has to commit, does that occur at least in part in Rockingham County,
such that there's venue in Rockingham County?
So is it disputed that venue would lie in Stratford?
That question's not before the court, Justice Donovan, and I'm not sure.
I mean, I think under our theory of venue it would, because there would be a sufficient portion of the offense that he committed here.
He delivered it occurred in Stratford?
I agree, yes, that part of the offense occurred in Stratford.
Part of the offense occurred somewhere that at least in this record we don't know, which is where he sent the communication from.
But from our perspective as a matter of common understanding of the word contact, part of the offense too occurred in Rockingham County
where she received the memorandum.
So would it be your position that if the protective party had driven down to Massachusetts,
opened the envelope in Massachusetts that venue would lie in either Massachusetts or Stratford?
So I think certainly under our theory of venue it would lie in Stratford.
It may well lie in Massachusetts, I'm pretty sure there's a federal overlay to this regime that the Violence Against Women Act
that may actually make that a federal offense, and so I can't speak specifically to that.
But I think under those circumstances at least part of the offense is happening in Stratford County
and venue would be appropriate there.
So fair to say your perspective is that venue could lie in multiple places?
Yes, Justice Countway, and I think that's consistent with what this court suggested in fortune.
It's really consistent with the plain language of the venue statute.
That if an element of the crime that the defendant commits occurs in multiple places,
then you can lie in both of those places.
And what is the significance of the location of the issuing court?
The court that issued the underlying order.
I'm not sure that that is...
I don't know that that's important for the purposes of this appeal based on the crimes they were charged
and the basis on which they were charged.
Isn't it inherently a contempt though?
I think you're right. I think that is the nature of it.
But it wasn't charged as a contempt, and I think that would be a different question.
If it were charged as contempt, I think they're very likely would be under the terms of the protective order venue
and it is Rockingham County. It's the 10th Circuit Court as well.
But because these were charged as violation of the protective order,
which I agree is akin to contempt but wasn't a criminal contempt charge in the underlying proceeding
and as stocking, I think that triggers the venue question that's before this court here.
So when the statute 173B in subsection 9, Roman numeral 2 makes reference to criminal contempt,
do you read that as a separate or different action than a statutory violation of a protective order charge?
I'm sorry, Your Honor, could you just point me to the provision again?
It says 173B 9 and then it's in Roman numeral 2.
Yes, Your Honor. I think that is contemplating that there are really two different charges here.
You can have charge for violation of a protective order.
You can also have charge for contempt of court, criminal contempt.
And I think I understand those to be really two separate things.
But I would certainly be fine if this court were to conclude this is basically just a contempt charge
and for that reason, you know, any reason on the record
and for that reason venue was appropriate in Rockingham 2.
I'm fine with that.
But my understanding of how this happened is it kind of, it was still the same issuing court
but it came as a separate criminal proceeding, not something that was a criminal contempt proceeding
that was initiated within the underlying protective order.
Understood.
How if at all does, so 173B 1 Roman 4 defines contact as any action to communicate?
How do you construe that any action, like how do, your argument as frames or revolves a lot around the definition of contact
but at least 173B which is different than the stocking statute, it says that contact means any action to communicate.
So I guess I'm just struggling a little bit with understanding how to construe that particularly in this context.
Her receiving it doesn't seem to be an action, well I don't know, I guess that's my question.
I would say it would be your honor and I think because the action to communicate is only completed,
the communication is only achieved once that she has received it.
And I would point the court to Dunbar for that because that really is, as I understand the proposition,
not for venue purposes but for what does contact mean in the Dunbar case.
So action would be, would there be an arc to action?
Action would start with the writing of the words, it would continue to the lawyer's receipt
and then continue to her ultimately reviewing, so that would all be an action?
It would your honor and I sense that the tension that you're seeing here may be the attenuation
but I would point the court to some other examples of where at least as I understand it under the defendants reading
there really wouldn't be venue in Rockingham County.
If this had been a phone call and it could have been a phone call from location unknown out of state
in another county and she received that phone call in Rockingham County and he said the exact same thing.
That's a much closer in time but it still is the transmission imparting a communication from one person to another
across county lines and I think under a common understanding of the word contact, communicate
and under a common understanding of the word action, that's all encompassed.
I don't think a person on the street or certainly turning to dictionary definitions would look at that
and say oh that somehow outside of the act that was being committed here, the offense that was being committed here
and I think a more extreme example less likely but I think highlights the same point.
If he had been on one side of a county line shouting across a county line
I think common understanding of the words used in the statute, all of that is part of the same action
of contact, communication with the victim.
But under your theory if the protected party for example didn't pick up the phone
there wouldn't be venue because she didn't receive the message in that county.
If the phone call was made from evidence, the phone call was made from Canada, there would be no venue
and the state wouldn't have any jurisdiction over an attempted violation of the order.
Well under the fact pattern here just to stand up and I think there would be jurisdiction at least in Stratford County.
There might not be jurisdiction for an attempt under those circumstances from outside of the order
if it were just coming directly in, if you couldn't find that it was intended
and in fact did pass through New Hampshire in some way
but that's probably, my understanding and I'm not an expert at this
that's where the federal overlay is designed to address
like that because it would be pretty easy to evade some form of liability
even under our construction by moving outside of the state
there still is going to be kind of a, it's not really privileges
it's really just a federal parliament at that point.
What function does action to in the sentence that Justice Will was quoting to you
what function does that language serve under your theory
because it seems to me that what you're arguing is that this turns on communication
and so aren't we, if we adopt your reading of the statute
aren't we reading out of the words action too?
I don't think so Justice School because as I understand that phrase
it really is as Justice Will was identifying
it's starting the communication, the course of the communication
that under Dungbars only achieved when the communications received
so it is, you have to do something to start it
if he threw some sort of communication to the winds
without any intent to get to her
and she somehow came across it
that may be too attenuated for him to engage in some action too
have it kind of land in her lap
but here when he is making the communication
I mean there may be a merits dispute
but there's certainly no dispute for the purposes of venue
that he was directing it to her attorney so that she would receive it
and she saw it, it's all part of that same course
and so I don't think it is limited to communication
but I think what Dungbar contemplates
and I honestly think what also Craig contemplates
is it's more than just sending the message
it is the completed communication that becomes the violation
maybe an attempt without that
but for it to be a violation, for it to be contact
it's the whole thing
You might have answered this already
forgive me if you did
but the sort of scenario where the facts are what they are
except that she retrieves the envelope
and opens it later when she's on a plane to Paris
so on your theory would you then cite venue in Stratford?
I think there would be a strong argument under our theory
that there would be a venue in Stratford
Sure
and I think if there weren't venue in Stratford
there may well be some other, like I said
the Violence Against Women Act may provide some sort of overlay
for federal prosecution
and if instead he sent it directly to her mailbox
in Portsmouth but she picked it up
stuck it in her purse, hopped on the plane
and read it on the plane
you would put venue in Portsmouth?
I think it could be in Portsmouth then too
because it's part of the course of that communication
where he directed it
there may be a question Your Honor
in that about intent
I mean intent remains
there is a mens rea component to this crime
and you would still have to actually be directing it to her
directing it to a particular place
but I don't think that's relevant for the venue question
But you could still bring a contempt charge
in the court that issued the original order
Sure Your Honor
I think you could
and I think that's consistent with the terms
of the protective order

but I don't understand the ability to bring a contempt charge

and really the question here

which I think is a slightly different analysis

in Stratford County

the victim's lawyer was her agent

initial_prompt: This is a New Hampshire Supreme Court oral argument. Preserve legal terminology, party names, docket numbers, and court names. Likely speakers include justices and counsel.
text:
This is case 2025-0349, state of New Hampshire versus Donald W. Little.

For the record, the Chief Justice is disqualified from this case.

Attorney Garland, I understand that you've reserved one minute for buttle.

Yes, Your Honor.

Thank you, and may it please the court. My name is Sam Garland, and I represent the state of New Hampshire in this case.

I want to start by talking about a couple of things that I don't think are in dispute, just to home in on the issue that's before the court.

The basic facts are undisputed. The trial court concluded there are undisputed facts for the purposes of the motion to dismiss,

and those facts are that the defendant is subject to a protective order, that he had some sort of arrangement.

I'm not sure if it was court ordered or if it was by agreement, but it's not relevant for the crimes before the court today,

that he would send alimony checks to his ex-wife's law office, the victim's law office in Dover,

and that she would then come there to collect the checks, and that the specific events that gave rise to the charges here

are that he wrote Liar Cheat Thief on the memorandum line of one of those checks.

She came to her attorney's law office in Dover, got the check, brought it back to her home in Rockingham County,

and saw that message when she got back to Rockingham County.

So I don't think those facts are in dispute, and they perform the basis of both of the charges.

I also don't think it's in dispute, in this case, that for both of the charges,

violation of the protective order and stalking, contact is an element of the offense that the defendant himself has to commit.

So he has to engage in contact in a way that violates either of those statutes for the purposes of criminal liability.

And so consistent with fortune, consistent with 6021, the question in this case, and really the only question before the court is,

is contact an element of the offense that he has to commit, does that occur at least in part in Rockingham County,

such that there's venue in Rockingham County?

So is it disputed that venue would lie in Stratford?

That question's not before the court, Justice Donovan, and I'm not sure.

I mean, I think under our theory of venue it would, because there would be a sufficient portion of the offense that he committed here.

He delivered it occurred in Stratford?

I agree, yes, that part of the offense occurred in Stratford.

Part of the offense occurred somewhere that at least in this record we don't know, which is where he sent the communication from.

But from our perspective as a matter of common understanding of the word contact, part of the offense too occurred in Rockingham County

where she received the memorandum.

So would it be your position that if the protective party had driven down to Massachusetts,

opened the envelope in Massachusetts that venue would lie in either Massachusetts or Stratford?

So I think certainly under our theory of venue it would lie in Stratford.

It may well lie in Massachusetts, I'm pretty sure there's a federal overlay to this regime that the Violence Against Women Act

that may actually make that a federal offense, and so I can't speak specifically to that.

But I think under those circumstances at least part of the offense is happening in Stratford County

and venue would be appropriate there.

So fair to say your perspective is that venue could lie in multiple places?

Yes, Justice Countway, and I think that's consistent with what this court suggested in fortune.

It's really consistent with the plain language of the venue statute.

That if an element of the crime that the defendant commits occurs in multiple places,

then you can lie in both of those places.

And what is the significance of the location of the issuing court?

The court that issued the underlying order.

I'm not sure that that is...

I don't know that that's important for the purposes of this appeal based on the crimes they were charged

and the basis on which they were charged.

Isn't it inherently a contempt though?

I think you're right. I think that is the nature of it.

But it wasn't charged as a contempt, and I think that would be a different question.

If it were charged as contempt, I think they're very likely would be under the terms of the protective order venue

and it is Rockingham County. It's the 10th Circuit Court as well.

But because these were charged as violation of the protective order,

which I agree is akin to contempt but wasn't a criminal contempt charge in the underlying proceeding

and as stocking, I think that triggers the venue question that's before this court here.

So when the statute 173B in subsection 9, Roman numeral 2 makes reference to criminal contempt,

do you read that as a separate or different action than a statutory violation of a protective order charge?

I'm sorry, Your Honor, could you just point me to the provision again?

It says 173B 9 and then it's in Roman numeral 2.

Yes, Your Honor. I think that is contemplating that there are really two different charges here.

You can have charge for violation of a protective order.

You can also have charge for contempt of court, criminal contempt.

And I think I understand those to be really two separate things.

But I would certainly be fine if this court were to conclude this is basically just a contempt charge

and for that reason, you know, any reason on the record

and for that reason venue was appropriate in Rockingham 2.

I'm fine with that.

But my understanding of how this happened is it kind of, it was still the same issuing court

but it came as a separate criminal proceeding, not something that was a criminal contempt proceeding

that was initiated within the underlying protective order.

Understood.

How if at all does, so 173B 1 Roman 4 defines contact as any action to communicate?

How do you construe that any action, like how do, your argument as frames or revolves a lot around the definition of contact

but at least 173B which is different than the stocking statute, it says that contact means any action to communicate.

So I guess I'm just struggling a little bit with understanding how to construe that particularly in this context.

Her receiving it doesn't seem to be an action, well I don't know, I guess that's my question.

I would say it would be your honor and I think because the action to communicate is only completed,

the communication is only achieved once that she has received it.

And I would point the court to Dunbar for that because that really is, as I understand the proposition,

not for venue purposes but for what does contact mean in the Dunbar case.

So action would be, would there be an arc to action?

Action would start with the writing of the words, it would continue to the lawyer's receipt

and then continue to her ultimately reviewing, so that would all be an action?

It would your honor and I sense that the tension that you're seeing here may be the attenuation

but I would point the court to some other examples of where at least as I understand it under the defendants reading
